skill.name: reviewing-pull-requests
skill.description: Guides pull request creation, review, quality gates, merge decisions, labeling, and reviewer assignment with GitHub-focused workflows.
skill.summary: Review, create, and manage pull requests with structured quality gates.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most markdown backtick and weak-cryptography alerts are false positives from examples, headings, and checklist text. The confirmed risk is legitimate PR automation that runs GitHub and git commands and can modify pull requests, reviewers, labels, and project boards. No prompt injection attempt, credential exfiltration, obfuscation, or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: GitHub CLI Automation Can Modify Repository State
medium_findings.description: The Python helper executes the GitHub CLI and git commands to create pull requests, apply labels, assign reviewers, inspect CODEOWNERS, and update project boards. The commands use argument arrays rather than shell concatenation, so command injection was not confirmed, but the script has real write-capable repository side effects when run with an authenticated GitHub CLI.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess pattern is real and the surrounding code shows authenticated GitHub operations with write effects. Confidence is below very high because the implementation avoids shell=True and no malicious destination or data exfiltration was found.

medium_findings.title: Quality Gate Script Runs External GitHub Commands
medium_findings.description: The shell helper calls gh and jq to fetch PR checks, changed files, diffs, additions, and deletions. Arguments are quoted and the behavior matches the skill purpose, but users should expect network access through GitHub CLI and local command execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external command use is directly visible and semantically tied to PR quality checks. It is a moderate operational risk, not a confirmed exploit, because command arguments are quoted and no shell-expanded untrusted command string is built.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Many static findings labeled as Ruby or shell backtick execution occur inside markdown fences, inline examples, and templates. These are documentation examples rather than executable code paths inside the skill runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown code fences or inline examples, not Ruby backtick execution. The context is instructional PR workflow text.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography flags appear on natural-language PR content such as descriptions, checklist labels, and review examples. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or any cryptographic API usage in the cited context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of representative flagged lines shows ordinary PR guidance, not crypto code. Confidence is high because no cryptographic operation appears at those locations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded GitHub URLs Are Illustrative
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are example pull request links in documentation. They do not cause network requests by themselves and do not point to suspicious collection endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both URLs are shown as PR output examples in markdown. No code at those lines performs an HTTP request.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Python subprocess.run invokes gh and git. The usage is purposeful and avoids shell=True, but it can perform authenticated repository actions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are present and execute external binaries. The risk is bounded by list-style arguments and lack of shell command string construction.

dangerous_patterns.title: GitHub CLI Network Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents and implements GitHub CLI workflows that contact GitHub and may change PR metadata. Users should run it only in repositories and accounts where those actions are intended.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The gh invocations are direct evidence of GitHub network operations. They appear aligned with the skill purpose and not with secret collection or exfiltration.

content.user_title: Review Pull Requests With Quality Gates
content.value_statement: Manual PR reviews can miss quality, test, and merge-readiness signals. This skill structures review, creation, labeling, and quality-gate workflows for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
actual_capabilities: Generates structured pull request titles and descriptions from branch and commit context.
Reviews pull requests using quality, security, testing, documentation, and merge-readiness checks.
Runs helper scripts for GitHub CLI based PR checks, labels, reviewer assignment, and project board sync.
Recommends merge, squash, or rebase strategies based on commit history and review status.
Uses included templates and best-practice references for consistent review output.
limitations: Requires GitHub CLI authentication for automated PR and project board actions.
Quality gate scripts provide heuristic checks and do not replace full CI, SAST, or human review.
The shell helper expects local tools such as gh and jq to be installed.
Reviewer and project assignment logic may need repository-specific customization.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Pull Request
use_cases.description: Create a clear PR title, description, testing summary, and related issue links before requesting review.

use_cases.title: Review a Pull Request
use_cases.description: Check code quality, tests, security concerns, documentation, and approval readiness in a repeatable format.

use_cases.title: Manage PR Workflow
use_cases.description: Apply labels, suggest reviewers, check CI status, and choose a merge strategy for active pull requests.

prompt_templates.title: Create a PR Description
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a pull request description for my current branch. Include summary, changes, testing, related issues, and breaking changes.

prompt_templates.title: Review a PR
prompt_templates.prompt: Review PR #123 for correctness, tests, security, documentation, and merge readiness. Give a decision and required changes.

prompt_templates.title: Run Quality Gates
prompt_templates.prompt: Run quality gate checks for PR #123 and summarize CI status, test coverage, security concerns, and PR size.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Merge Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze PR #123, its commits, review status, and branch state. Recommend merge, squash, or rebase with reasoning and risks.

output_examples.input: Create a PR for my authentication branch.
output_examples.output: A PR draft with a conventional title, summary, change list, testing checklist, related issue section, and breaking-change note.

output: Review summary with strengths and issues.
Quality gate results for CI, tests, security, and size.
Decision such as approved, commented, or changes requested.

output_examples.input: Should I squash or merge this PR?
output_examples.output: A merge recommendation based on commit quality, PR size, review state, and desired repository history.

best_practices: Run automated checks before posting final review feedback.
Keep PRs small enough for reviewers to understand the change quickly.
Verify GitHub CLI context before running scripts that modify PR metadata.
anti_patterns: Approving a PR when CI, tests, or critical review comments are unresolved.
Using automation to assign reviewers or projects without checking repository conventions.
Treating heuristic security checks as a complete vulnerability assessment.

faq.question: Does this skill create pull requests?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide PR creation and includes a helper script that uses GitHub CLI.

faq.question: Can it post review decisions?
faq.answer: It can prepare review decisions and workflows. Posting depends on available GitHub permissions and user approval.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: Yes, when GitHub CLI commands are used to inspect or modify pull requests.

faq.question: Is it safe to run on private repositories?
faq.answer: Use normal repository safeguards. Review the commands first and run them only with the intended GitHub account.

faq.question: Does it replace human review?
faq.answer: No. It structures checks and highlights risks, but a human reviewer should make final approval decisions.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
